title: Best Air Fryers of 2025: Tested & Ranked for Every Kitchen
excerpt: Air fryers have transformed home cooking, delivering crispy, restaurant-quality results with up to 75% less oil than traditional deep frying. After hands-on testing of 20+ models across capacity, cook speed, ease of cleaning, and value, we found the top six air fryers worth buying in 2025. Whether you're cooking for one or feeding a family of six, there's a perfect pick here.
author: CompareElite Team

products.name: Ninja Foodi DualZone XL 10-qt Air Fryer (DZ401)
pros: Two independent 5-qt baskets let you cook two foods at completely different times and temperatures
Smart Finish and Match Cook modes sync both zones to finish simultaneously
Massive 10-qt total capacity comfortably feeds 4 to 6 people
Six versatile cooking functions: Air Fry, Air Broil, Roast, Bake, Reheat, and Dehydrate
Dishwasher-safe baskets and crisper plates make cleanup effortless
cons: Larger footprint requires significant counter space
Nonstick coating may show wear after extended heavy use
Higher price point compared to single-basket models
products.description: The Ninja DZ401 is the gold standard for family-sized air frying. Its DualZone Technology sets it apart: two completely independent 5-quart baskets let you cook chicken wings at 400°F while simultaneously roasting vegetables at 375°F. The Smart Finish feature automatically staggers cooking start times so both foods finish at exactly the same moment. In testing, it produced the crispiest fries and most evenly cooked chicken of any model we tried. The 10-quart total capacity can handle a full rack of ribs or two separate dinner components at once. It's larger than most, but for households cooking for four or more people, it's unmatched.

products.name: Cosori Pro Gen 2 Air Fryer 5.8QT
pros: 13 one-touch cooking presets cover virtually every air frying scenario
Square 5.8-qt basket fits a whole 5-lb chicken or full batch of fries
Built-in NTC sensor maintains precise temperature within ±3°F
Comes with 100 chef-crafted recipes in the companion app
Dishwasher-safe removable basket and crisper plate
cons: App requires account creation to access all recipes
Touchscreen can be slightly unresponsive in humid environments
products.description: Cosori's Pro Gen 2 delivers flagship-level performance at a mid-range price, making it our top value pick. The upgraded version features improved heat distribution and a sleeker design compared to its predecessor. The 13 preset programs include dedicated settings for steak, seafood, frozen foods, and even desserts — all optimized by Cosori's culinary team. The NTC thermal sensor is a standout: it continuously monitors internal temperature and auto-adjusts to prevent hot spots, resulting in noticeably more even cooking than competitors at this price. The square basket is particularly practical since it maximizes usable cooking area versus round alternatives. For solo cooks or couples, this is the best combination of capability and value available.

products.name: COSORI TurboBlaze 6-Qt Air Fryer
pros: TurboBlaze technology preheats in under 2 minutes — faster than any competitor tested
90°F to 450°F temperature range covers everything from proofing bread dough to searing
9 cooking functions including a dedicated Proof mode for baking
PFAS-free ceramic-coated basket is healthier and easier to clean
Precise 1°F temperature increments for exact cooking control
cons: Dark gray color shows grease splatters more readily than lighter models
Slightly louder fan than the Pro Gen 2
Fewer preset programs than competitors at the same price
products.description: The TurboBlaze earns its name. In our testing, it reached target temperature 40% faster than the category average, cutting total cook time on frozen french fries from 18 minutes to under 13. The ceramic-coated basket is a meaningful upgrade — it's PFAS-free, releases food more easily than standard nonstick, and doesn't degrade with metal utensils. The 90°F minimum temperature also opens up uses that most air fryers can't handle: proofing bread dough, making yogurt, and slow-warming leftovers without drying them out. If speed and versatility are priorities and you're cooking for two to four people, the TurboBlaze is the most efficient choice in this group.

products.name: Instant Vortex Plus 6-Qt Air Fryer
pros: Simple, intuitive one-touch programs require no learning curve
EvenCrisp 360° airflow technology delivers consistent results every time
Quiet operation — among the quietest models we tested
Compact design fits comfortably on most kitchen counters
Backed by Instant Pot's well-established brand support and warranty
cons: Fewer advanced features and customization options than rivals
6-function limitation means no dedicated dehydrate or broil mode
Interior LED light is dim and makes it hard to monitor food
products.description: The Instant Vortex Plus is the easiest air fryer to use right out of the box — and it stays that way. Where competitors load you with dozens of settings and connectivity apps, the Vortex Plus gives you six clearly labeled programs: Air Fry, Bake, Roast, Broil, Dehydrate, and Reheat. Press one button and it works. The EvenCrisp technology delivers genuinely good results: in our testing, frozen chicken nuggets came out uniformly golden with no cold spots. It's slightly quieter than other models too, which matters in open-plan kitchens. For someone who wants great air-fried food without a steep learning curve — or for a gift — this is the perfect entry point.

products.name: Philips Premium Airfryer XXL (HD9650/96)
pros: 7-quart capacity handles a 3-lb chicken, full rack of ribs, or 2.5 lbs of fries in one batch
Fat Removal Technology drains and captures grease below the basket for genuinely healthier results
Rapid Air circulation achieves even browning on all surfaces without manual shaking
5 preset programs plus keep-warm mode via the HomeID app
Premium build quality with a sleek digital display — among the most durable we've tested
cons: Premium price is the highest in this roundup for a single-basket model
Bulkier than similarly priced competitors — needs more cabinet space
App connectivity features add complexity beginners may not need
products.description: Philips invented the air fryer category, and the Premium XXL shows why they remain the benchmark. The 7-quart capacity is the largest single-basket option in our roundup — large enough to air fry a whole chicken in 30 minutes or 2.5 lbs of fries in one uninterrupted batch. The proprietary Fat Removal Technology is genuinely innovative: a sloped basket floor channels released fat away from the food and collects it in a separate tray, reducing calorie content measurably compared to standard air frying. In our taste tests, chicken cooked in the Philips ranked highest for texture and flavour. The build quality also stands out — the housing, controls, and basket all feel substantially more durable than the competition. If you're cooking large portions regularly and want the best possible results, this is the one.

products.name: Ninja AF161 Max XL Air Fryer
pros: MaxCrisp Technology reaches 450°F — the highest temperature in this roundup
5.5-quart basket comfortably handles 3 lbs of fries or a whole rotisserie chicken
Six cooking functions including a dedicated dehydrate mode
High-gloss finish looks premium on any countertop
Fan works up to 30% faster at 450°F than the AF100 predecessor
cons: Higher wattage makes it one of the more energy-intensive models
No dual-zone or smart connectivity features
Older design compared to newer Ninja releases with updated interiors
products.description: The Ninja AF161 Max XL exists for one purpose: maximum crisp. Its 450°F MaxCrisp Technology delivers superheated air that produces a fried texture closer to deep-fat frying than any other basket air fryer we tested. Frozen foods that typically take 20 minutes at 400°F are done in 14 minutes at 450°F, and the result is noticeably crunchier. The 5.5-quart basket is large enough for most family portions without the bulk of a dual-basket model, and the six cooking programs cover all the basics. Where it falls short is connectivity and multitasking — there's no app, no dual zone, and no smart features. But if your priority is the crispiest possible results fast, the AF161 Max XL delivers better than anything else at its price.

sections.heading: Capacity: How Much Do You Actually Need?
sections.content: Air fryer capacity is measured in quarts, and the right size depends entirely on how many people you're regularly cooking for. For 1-2 people, a 3-4 quart model is sufficient and takes up less counter space. For 3-4 people, aim for 5-6 quarts to cook full portions in one batch. Families of 5 or more should look at 7+ quart models or dual-basket options like the Ninja DZ401 that offer 10 quarts total. Resist the urge to go too big — oversized models waste energy on small portions and the food cooks less evenly when the basket isn't reasonably full.

sections.heading: Temperature Range and Speed
sections.content: Most air fryers operate between 200°F and 400°F, which covers nearly everything from reheating leftovers to crisping wings. Models that reach 450°F (like the Ninja AF161) produce noticeably crunchier results on frozen foods. At the low end, models with 90°F minimum (like the TurboBlaze) unlock baking uses like proofing bread dough. Preheating time varies significantly: budget models take 5-8 minutes while premium options with faster heating elements are ready in under 2 minutes.

sections.heading: Basket Material: Nonstick vs. Ceramic
sections.content: The vast majority of air fryers use a PTFE (Teflon-style) nonstick coating. It's effective but can degrade over time, especially if scratched with metal utensils. Ceramic-coated baskets (like the TurboBlaze) are PFAS-free, more durable with metal utensils, and easier to hand-wash. The tradeoff is that ceramic coatings require lower cooking sprays — oil-based cooking sprays can damage them. If health concerns around traditional nonstick are a factor, ceramic is worth the slight premium.

sections.heading: Ease of Cleaning
sections.content: All six models in this roundup have dishwasher-safe baskets — but dishwasher cleaning can degrade nonstick coatings faster than hand-washing. The key factors for real-world cleaning ease are basket shape (square baskets have fewer hard-to-reach corners), removable crisper plates, and how quickly grease builds up on interior walls. The Philips XXL with Fat Removal Technology requires the least cleaning effort since grease drains away from food automatically.

faq.q: What size air fryer do I need for a family of 4?
faq.a: For a family of four, you'll want at least a 5-6 quart single-basket air fryer or a dual-basket model with 8-10 quarts total. This lets you cook a full pound of fries or 4 chicken thighs in a single batch without overcrowding, which is critical for achieving crispy results. Overcrowding the basket forces food to steam rather than air fry, resulting in soft textures. Our top pick for families is the Ninja DZ401 with dual 5-qt baskets.

faq.q: Are air fryers actually healthier than deep frying?
faq.a: Yes — significantly so. Traditional deep frying submerges food in 375°F oil, which is absorbed into the food. Air fryers use circulating hot air with 1-2 teaspoons of oil (or none at all), reducing fat content by 70-80% compared to deep frying. The Philips Premium XXL takes this further with Fat Removal Technology that actively drains grease away from the food during cooking. The resulting calorie count is dramatically lower while texture and flavour remain comparable for most foods.

faq.q: Can I use aluminum foil or parchment paper in an air fryer?
faq.a: Yes, with important caveats. Both are safe to use, but you must never cover the entire basket bottom — you need airflow underneath food to achieve the crispy results air fryers are known for. Use parchment paper or foil only as a liner beneath the food, leaving the sides and edges of the basket uncovered. Never preheat with foil or parchment in the basket without food on top, as the lightweight material can get blown into the heating element and cause a fire.

faq.q: How does an air fryer compare to a convection oven?
faq.a: Air fryers are essentially compact convection ovens with a more powerful fan. Because the cooking chamber is much smaller, hot air circulates faster and more intensively around food, typically producing crispier results in 20-30% less time than a convection oven. Convection ovens win on capacity — you can cook a full turkey or multiple sheet pans simultaneously. Air fryers win on speed, crisp texture, energy efficiency, and countertop convenience. For everyday cooking tasks (fries, wings, reheating pizza, roasting vegetables), an air fryer is the better tool.

faq.q: Do I need to preheat an air fryer?
faq.a: For best results, yes — but preheating time is short. Most models reach target temperature in 2-5 minutes. Preheating ensures the cooking environment is at the right temperature the moment food goes in, leading to more consistent browning, better texture, and more accurate cooking times. Skip preheating only for longer cook times (20+ minutes) where the difference is negligible. All models in this roundup have a preheat function or reach temperature quickly enough that it's not a burden.

faq.q: What foods should I NOT cook in an air fryer?
faq.a: Avoid anything with a wet batter (beer-battered fish, tempura) — the batter will drip through the basket before it sets, making a mess. Large leafy vegetables like spinach or kale will blow around from the fan and burn unevenly. Very delicate pastries with loose toppings (some cakes, open-faced cheesy dishes) can get disrupted by airflow. Fresh cheese used as a topping will melt through the basket. Whole roasts heavier than about 5 lbs won't fit properly in most models. Everything else — frozen foods, proteins, vegetables, and even desserts — works extremely well.

top_picks.name: Ninja Foodi DualZone XL (DZ401)
top_picks.reason: Best overall — dual-basket flexibility, massive capacity, and the most versatile cooking options make it worth every dollar for households of 3 or more.

top_picks.name: Cosori Pro Gen 2 5.8QT
top_picks.reason: Best value — precise temperature control, 13 smart presets, and a square basket that maximizes cooking area at a price under $120.

top_picks.name: Philips Premium Airfryer XXL
top_picks.reason: Best results — the original air fryer brand delivers the most evenly cooked food with Fat Removal Technology that genuinely reduces calories versus standard air frying.
